Chihuahua: A Chihuahua is a tiny dog with a compact body, a large apple-shaped head, and prominent, luminous eyes.
A Chihuahua looks like a miniature canine, featuring either a smooth, glossy coat or a long, soft one, and characteristically large, erect ears.
A Chihuahua is an exceptionally small dog with slender legs, a slightly arched back, and a sickle-shaped tail carried high.
A Chihuahua appears as a delicate, fine-boned dog with a short muzzle and an alert, saucy expression.
A Chihuahua is a toy-sized dog defined by its disproportionately large, flared ears set on a domed skull.
A Chihuahua looks like a pocket-sized companion with a fragile frame, often seen in colors like fawn, black, cream, or chocolate.
A Chihuahua is a diminutive dog with a level topline and a tail that loops over its back or to the side.
A Chihuahua has a distinct appearance with a well-rounded 'apple dome' skull and full, round eyes that are set well apart.
A Chihuahua is a very small dog, whose body is just slightly longer than it is tall, giving it a compact look.
A Chihuahua looks like a dainty yet bold dog, with either a short, sleek coat or a long coat with fringes on the ears and legs.
You can identify a Chihuahua primarily by its extremely small size, often weighing no more than 6 pounds.
You can identify a Chihuahua by its distinctive apple-domed skull, a key hallmark of the breed's visual standard.
You can identify a Chihuahua by its very large, erect ears that flare out to the sides, resembling bat wings.
Identify a Chihuahua by its large, round, expressive eyes that are typically very dark, though sometimes ruby or luminous.
Look for a tiny, compact body with fine-boned legs to identify a Chihuahua.
You can identify a Chihuahua by its unique sickle-shaped tail, which is often carried up and over its back.
Identify a smooth-coat Chihuahua by its glossy, close-lying fur, or a long-coat by its soft, feathered hair.
A key identifier for a Chihuahua is the 90-degree angle where its short muzzle meets its forehead, known as the 'stop'.
You can identify a Chihuahua by its alert and swift, terrier-like demeanor packed into a tiny body.
Identify a Chihuahua by observing its overall fragile and delicate bone structure, distinguishing it from other small breeds.

Japanese Spaniel: A Japanese Spaniel, also known as a Japanese Chin, is a small, elegant toy dog with a distinctively flattened face and large, wide-set eyes.
A Japanese Spaniel looks like a dainty, aristocratic dog with a profuse, long, and silky coat, typically black and white or red and white.
It is a brachycephalic breed with a broad head, a very short muzzle, and a slightly undershot jaw.
A Japanese Spaniel has a cobby body, a high-set, plumed tail that arches gracefully over its back, and a surprised yet charming expression.
This dog looks like a noble lapdog, with feathered ears, a mane-like ruff around its neck, and feathered 'pants' on its hind legs.
A Japanese Spaniel has a unique, stylish gait, lifting its feet high when it moves.
It is characterized by its large, dark, round eyes that are set far apart, contributing to its oriental look.
A Japanese Spaniel has a single coat of straight, silky hair that is soft to the touch and requires regular grooming.
The dog appears square in proportion, with a compact frame and fine-boned legs.
A Japanese Spaniel looks like a living piece of art, with symmetrical facial markings being highly prized, often including a spot or line on its forehead.
You can identify a Japanese Spaniel by its short, broad, and flattened muzzle, a classic brachycephalic feature.
Identify it by the large, dark, wide-set eyes that give it a look of astonishment and inquiry.
Look for the abundant, silky, single coat, which is most often seen in a striking black and white pattern.
You can identify a Japanese Spaniel by its plumed tail, which is carried high and arches completely over its back.

The presence of a thick ruff or mane around the neck and chest is a distinguishing visual trait.
You can identify a Japanese Spaniel by its high-stepping, stylish trot.
Look for the distinct 'oriental' expression, created by the combination of its flat face, wide eyes, and the white showing in the inner corners of the eyes.

The contrast between its white coat and colored patches (black, red, or sable) is a strong visual identifier.

Maltese Dog: A Maltese dog is a small, toy breed completely covered in a long, flat, and silky mantle of pure white hair.
A Maltese dog looks like a graceful and refined canine, with a slightly rounded skull, a dark, button-like nose, and very dark, alert eyes.
It has a compact, fine-boned body, with its length being roughly equal to its height, giving it a square appearance.
The Maltese dog is characterized by its long, hanging ears which are heavily feathered and often blend into the coat of the neck and shoulders.
It has a well-plumed tail that curves gracefully over its back, with the long hair falling to one side.
A Maltese dog's coat is a single layer of hair without an undercoat, which hangs straight and heavy to the ground.
This dog has a distinctive look due to the sharp contrast between its pure white coat and its black 'points'—the eye rims, nose, and lips.
It has a lively, spirited expression, enhanced by its dark, oval-shaped eyes.
The dog's movement is flowing and effortless, appearing as if it's floating across the ground under its long white coat.
A Maltese dog looks like a small, elegant cloud of white, with a gentle yet playful demeanor visible in its features.
You can identify a Maltese dog by its floor-length, single layer coat of pure, brilliant white hair.
Identify it by the striking black points: the jet-black nose, eye rims, and lips that stand out against the white fur.
Look for the long, heavily feathered ears that hang close to its head.
The long, plumed tail carried in a graceful arch over its back is a key identifier.
Its compact, square-proportioned body, hidden beneath the long hair, is a characteristic of the breed.
You can identify a Maltese by its lack of an undercoat; the hair is silky and straight, not woolly or curly.
The very dark, intelligent, and oval-shaped eyes give the Maltese a gentle and alert expression that is easy to recognize.
Its smooth, flowing, and seemingly floating gait is a distinguishing feature.

Pekinese: A Pekinese is a small, stocky, and muscular dog, low to the ground, with a distinctive rolling gait.
A Pekinese looks like a miniature lion, with a long, thick double coat and a prominent ruff or mane around its neck.
It has a brachycephalic (short-muzzled) face, a broad, flat head, and large, dark, lustrous eyes set wide apart.
The Pekinese is characterized by a V-shaped wrinkle over its short, broad nose, which sits between its eyes.
This dog has a heavy-fronted body with lighter hindquarters, short, bowed legs, and a high-set tail, thickly plumed and arched over the back.
Its coat is long and coarse with a soft, thick undercoat, and can come in various colors, most commonly shades of gold, red, or sable.
A Pekinese has heart-shaped ears that lie flat against its head, heavily feathered so they blend in with the mane.
It has a bold, self-important expression, reinforced by its confident and deliberate rolling walk.
The dog's face is very flat, and the hair on the face grows in a way that accentuates this feature.
A Pekinese looks like a dignified, compact dog with an abundance of hair and a pear-shaped body.
You can identify a Pekinese by its short, stocky, low-to-the-ground build and its distinct rolling gait.
Look for the prominent 'lion's mane'—a long, profuse ruff around its neck and shoulders.
Identify it by its very flat face, broad muzzle, and the characteristic skin fold or wrinkle over its nose.
The large, dark, and slightly prominent eyes, set very far apart, are a key identifier.
Its body shape is unique: heavier in the front with massive shoulders and a narrower rear, contributing to its roll.
The long, coarse outer coat with a thick, soft undercoat is a defining visual trait.
You can identify a Pekinese by its heavily plumed tail that is set high and carried arched over its back.
Look for its short, bowed forelegs, which are a characteristic feature of the breed's structure.
Its heart-shaped, feathered ears that lie flat against the skull are a subtle but important identifier.
The dignified, almost arrogant expression and deliberate pace are strong visual and behavioral identifiers.

Shih-Tzu: A Shih-Tzu is a small, sturdy toy dog with a long, flowing double coat that covers its entire body.
A Shih-Tzu looks like a little lion dog, characterized by its short, brachycephalic muzzle, large, dark, round eyes, and a distinctly arrogant carriage.
It has a warm, sweet expression, often enhanced by the hair on its head being tied up in a topknot to keep it out of its eyes.
The Shih-Tzu has a body that is slightly longer than it is tall, with a broad, deep chest and a level topline.
This dog is known for its heavily plumed tail that is set high and curls gaily over its back.
Its coat is dense and luxurious, with a good undercoat, and comes in a wide variety of colors and patterns.
A Shih-Tzu has large, pendant ears that are so heavily coated with hair they blend seamlessly with the coat of the head and neck.
It has a characteristic undershot bite, where the lower jaw is slightly longer than the upper jaw, though the teeth should not show when the mouth is closed.
The hair on the bridge of the nose grows upwards, giving a distinctive 'chrysanthemum' effect to the face.
A Shih-Tzu looks like a proud and lively little dog, moving with a smooth, flowing, and jaunty stride.
You can identify a Shih-Tzu by its long, flowing double coat that often reaches the floor.
Look for the hair on top of its head, which is commonly tied up into a decorative topknot.
Identify it by its short, square muzzle and the upward-growing hair on its nose, creating a 'chrysanthemum' face.
The large, round, dark eyes, set wide apart and looking straight ahead, are a key identifier.
Its heavily plumed tail curving over its back is a hallmark of the breed.
You can identify a Shih-Tzu by its distinctly undershot jaw, a correct feature for the breed.
The sturdy, compact body that is longer than it is tall helps to distinguish it from similar breeds like the Lhasa Apso.
Look for its proud, head-high carriage and smooth, flowing gait.
The very long, drop ears that are heavily covered in hair are a distinguishing feature.
Identify it by its overall 'living plush toy' appearance, with a sweet and outgoing expression.

Blenheim Spaniel: A Blenheim Spaniel is a toy spaniel with a silky, medium-length coat featuring rich chestnut markings on a pearly white background.
A Blenheim Spaniel looks like a gentle, graceful dog with large, dark, round eyes that convey a soft and melting expression.
It has a slightly rounded head, a conical muzzle, and long, high-set ears covered in silky feathering.
This dog is characterized by its well-broken, distinct red-and-white patches, with a desirable 'Blenheim spot' or 'lozenge'—a chestnut marking on top of the head.
A Blenheim Spaniel has a well-proportioned, compact body with a deep chest and a tail that is carried level with its back.
Its coat is of moderate length, with notable feathering on the ears, chest, legs, and tail, but is free from curl.
The face of a Blenheim Spaniel is sweet and endearing, with well-cushioned lips and a black nose.
It has a refined bone structure and an elegant, free-moving gait.
The markings on the head are symmetrical, framing the gentle face with patches of chestnut over each ear and eye.
A Blenheim Spaniel looks like a small, noble companion, embodying both the spirit of a spaniel and the size of a toy breed.
You can identify a Blenheim Spaniel by its distinct color pattern: rich chestnut markings on a clear, pearly white coat.
Look for the highly-prized 'Blenheim spot,' a small, solid chestnut marking located in the center of the white blaze on its forehead.
Identify it by its large, round, dark brown eyes that give it a characteristically soft and gentle expression.
The long, high-set, and heavily feathered ears are a key visual feature of this spaniel.
Its silky coat of moderate length, with feathering on the legs, chest, and tail, is a strong identifier.
You can identify a Blenheim Spaniel by its elegant and aristocratic appearance, combined with a friendly demeanor.

Papillon: A Papillon is a small, fine-boned toy dog distinguished by its large, butterfly-like ears that are erect and fringed with long hair.
A Papillon looks like an elegant and dainty dog, with a long, silky, flowing single coat that is typically white with patches of any color.
It has a lively, intelligent expression, with dark, medium-sized, almond-shaped eyes.
The breed is characterized by its long, plumed tail that is set high and arches over its back, with the hair cascading down its side.
A Papillon has a slightly rounded skull between the ears and a fine, tapering muzzle.
Its body is slightly longer than it is tall, giving it a rectangular outline.
The coat is abundant and straight, with a frill on the chest, and feathering on the ears, the back of the legs, and the tail.
This dog moves with a light, quick, and graceful gait, appearing effortless.
The name 'Papillon', meaning butterfly in French, refers directly to the signature look of its fringed, upright ears.
A Papillon looks alert and spirited, a big dog personality in a small, delicate-looking package.
You can identify a Papillon primarily by its large, erect, and well-fringed ears that resemble the wings of a butterfly.
Look for the long, silky, single coat, which is predominantly white with patches of color.
Identify it by its high-set, profusely plumed tail that arches over the body.
The fine-boned, dainty, and elegant body structure is a key identifier.
Its alert, lively, and intelligent expression, conveyed through its dark, almond-shaped eyes, is a distinguishing feature.
You can identify a Papillon by its light and graceful trot, which looks effortless.
Look for a white blaze on the face and a colored patch over each eye and ear, which is a classic marking pattern.
The 'Phalène' is a drop-eared variety, but the erect-eared 'Papillon' is the most recognizable form.
Its single coat, lacking an undercoat, gives it a sleek yet profusely haired appearance.
Identify it by its overall look of delicate, yet resilient, elegance.
A Papillon is a small, fine-boned dog whose most striking feature is its large, erect ears fringed with long hair, resembling a butterfly's wings.

Its body is slightly longer than it is tall, and it moves with a quick, light-footed grace.
The coat has a distinct frill of longer hair on the chest and feathering on the back of its legs.
It has a single coat of hair, with no undercoat, which is long, straight, and abundant.
A Papillon looks like a perpetually happy and curious dog, with a sprightly and spirited demeanor.
The classic markings include colored patches covering both ears and eyes, separated by a white blaze.
It has a delicate appearance but is a hardy and athletic little dog.

Toy Terrier: A Toy Terrier is a small, elegant, and slender dog with a sleek, short, and glossy coat, typically in black and tan.
A Toy Terrier looks like a miniature version of a larger terrier, possessing a compact, well-proportioned body and a spirited expression.
It is characterized by its long, narrow, wedge-shaped head and large, erect, candle-flame-shaped ears.
This dog has dark, almond-shaped eyes that are full of fire and intelligence.
A Toy Terrier has a level topline, a deep, narrow chest, and a graceful arch over its loin.
Its coat is short, close, and glossy, with distinct, rich tan markings on a jet-black body.
The tan markings are clearly defined, appearing over the eyes, on the muzzle, chest, inside the legs, and under the tail.
It has a fine-boned structure with long, graceful legs, giving it an appearance of refined athleticism.
The tail is traditionally docked to a short length, carried high, but natural tails are also seen.
A Toy Terrier looks alert and poised, always seeming ready for action despite its small size.
You can identify a Toy Terrier by its diminutive size combined with classic terrier features.
Look for the distinctive black and tan coat pattern, with a glossy black body and rich, well-defined tan points.
The large, erect ears, shaped like a candle flame and set high on the head, are a key identifier.
Identify it by its long, wedge-shaped head and fine, tapering muzzle.
Its sleek, fine-boned, and elegant body structure, with a noticeable arch over the loin, is a distinguishing feature.
The short, smooth, and glossy coat that lies close to the skin is a strong visual trait.
You can identify it by its fiery, intelligent expression seen in its dark, almond-shaped eyes.
The clean lines and graceful, athletic build separate it from other toy breeds.
Look for the precise placement of the tan markings, particularly the small tan 'thumbprints' on the pasterns and a tan spot on each cheek.
Its overall appearance of a scaled-down Manchester Terrier is a primary method of identification.
A Toy Terrier has a small, slender, and elegant body with a short, glossy black and tan coat.
It looks like a miniature Doberman or Manchester Terrier, with a wedge-shaped head and fine bones.

The coat is jet black with very specific, rich tan markings above the eyes, on the muzzle, chest, and legs.
It has a compact and muscular form, but maintains an overall impression of refinement.
The dog looks poised and energetic, with a high head and tail carriage.
Its skull is long and flat, tapering to a fine muzzle, creating a distinct head shape.
A Toy Terrier has a look of quintessential terrier spirit—bold and curious—in a tiny, polished package.

Rhodesian Ridgeback: A Rhodesian Ridgeback is a large, muscular, and athletic dog, known for the distinctive ridge of hair along its back that grows in the opposite direction to the rest of its coat.
A Rhodesian Ridgeback looks powerful and well-balanced, with a short, dense, and glossy coat ranging in color from light wheaten to red wheaten.
The defining ridge is clearly visible, starting behind the shoulders and tapering to a point by the hips, and must contain two identical 'crowns' or whorls.
It has a strong, flat-topped head of fair length, a powerful muzzle, and a black or brown nose that corresponds with its eye color.
This dog has round, intelligent eyes that are set moderately well apart and express a dignified, calm confidence.
A Rhodesian Ridgeback has medium-sized, pendant ears that are set rather high and carried close to the head.
Its body is strong and deep-chested, with a powerful, muscular back and limbs, built for stamina and speed.
The tail is thick at the base and tapers to a point, carried with a slight upward curve.
It has a symmetrical and well-muscled appearance, conveying a sense of contained power and agility.
A Rhodesian Ridgeback looks like a noble and formidable hound, with a quiet and steady gaze.
You can identify a Rhodesian Ridgeback unequivocally by the prominent ridge of backward-growing hair on its spine.
Look for the two symmetrical whorls or 'crowns' at the top of the ridge, which are a required feature.
Identify it by its strong, athletic, and well-muscled build, standing over two feet tall at the shoulder.
The short, sleek coat in a shade of wheaten (from light to reddish) is a key identifier.
Its powerful but dignified appearance, combined with a calm and intelligent expression, is a strong visual cue.
You can identify it by its deep chest and strong, straight front legs.
Look for its round, bright eyes, which should harmonize with the coat color (dark eyes with a black nose, amber eyes with a brown nose).
Its medium-sized, triangular drop ears that hang close to the head are a distinguishing feature.
The dog's powerful, free, and efficient gait is another way to identify it.
Identify it by its overall look of a capable and enduring hound, built for the African bush.
A Rhodesian Ridgeback has a large, muscular body with a short, wheaten-colored coat.
Its most unique visual feature is the 'ridge'—a strip of hair along its spine growing in the opposite direction.
This dog looks strong and athletic, with a deep chest, powerful legs, and a noble head carriage.
The ridge on its back must have two identical crowns (whorls) directly opposite each other at the top.
It has a calm and intelligent expression in its round, dark or amber eyes.
A Rhodesian Ridgeback has a sleek, glossy coat that ranges in color from light wheaten to a reddish-gold.
It has a strong, slightly tapering tail and medium-sized ears that hang down.
The dog has a well-balanced and symmetrical appearance, looking both powerful and agile.

Afghan Hound: An Afghan Hound is a tall, slender sighthound defined by its thick, long, and silky coat that covers its entire body.
An Afghan Hound looks aristocratic and dignified, with a long, narrow head, a prominent Roman nose, and a topknot of long, fine hair.
It is a dog of striking contrasts: powerful and elegant, with prominent hipbones, a deep chest, and a fine-boned structure.
An Afghan Hound has a unique tail that is low-set and ends in a distinctive ring or curl.
This hound has large, well-padded paws, which are a surprising feature for such a gracefully built dog.
Its coat can be any color, but the texture is always fine and silky, resembling human hair.
An Afghan Hound carries its head high with an aloof, almost exotic expression in its dark, almond-shaped eyes.
It has a powerful, springy, and smooth gait that showcases its speed and agility.
The dog's long, pendulous ears are covered in even longer, silky hair, blending seamlessly into the rest of its coat.
An Afghan Hound looks like a supermodel from the canine world, with a majestic and statuesque presence.
You can identify an Afghan Hound primarily by its incredibly long, fine, and flowing coat, which is unlike any other breed.
Look for the distinct topknot of silky hair on its head and the long, feathered ears.
Identify it by its prominent, high hipbones, a key feature of its unique anatomical structure.
The low-set tail with a ring-like curve at the end is a hallmark identifier of the Afghan Hound.
Its long, refined, and narrow head with a slightly Roman nose is a distinguishing facial feature.
You can identify an Afghan Hound by its aloof, dignified, and somewhat distant expression.
The combination of a tall, slender, elegant body with a powerful, elastic gait is a key characteristic.
Look for its large, almost snowshoe-like paws, which provide traction in its native mountainous terrain.
The overall silhouette, with its deep chest, tucked-up loin, and unique topline, is a strong identifier.
Identify it by its regal and majestic carriage; it moves with a sense of purpose and grace.
An Afghan Hound has a tall, slender body draped in a long, flowing mantle of fine, silky hair.
It looks like an ancient aristocrat with its long, narrow head, distant gaze, and a stylish topknot of hair.
This dog has a very distinctive shape, with high, prominent hipbones and a deep, powerful chest.
An Afghan Hound's visual signature is its tail, which is sparsely feathered and forms a tight ring at the tip.
It has surprisingly large paws for its delicate build, and long, low-set ears covered in flowing hair.
The dog looks graceful and exotic, moving with a powerful, springy stride that makes its long coat float.
Its eyes are almond-shaped and dark, conveying an expression of aloof intelligence.
An Afghan Hound's coat can be any color, and its texture is incredibly fine and luxurious.
It looks like a creature of both speed and beauty, with the musculature of a sighthound hidden beneath its glamorous coat.
The overall impression is one of dramatic, statuesque elegance and dignified reserve.

Basset Hound: A Basset Hound is a short-legged, long-bodied, and heavy-boned dog, famous for its exceptionally long, velvety, drooping ears.
A Basset Hound looks perpetually sad or solemn, due to its droopy, diamond-shaped eyes with visible, reddish lower lids (haws).
It has a large, domed head with a significant amount of loose, wrinkled skin, particularly around the brow and neck (dewlap).
This hound has a short, dense, and smooth coat, most commonly seen in a classic tri-color pattern of black, tan, and white.
Its legs are very short and powerful, often with wrinkled ankles and large, massive paws that turn slightly outwards.
A Basset Hound has a long, tapering tail that is carried high and curved gently over its back, often with a white tip.
The dog's body is substantial and long, giving it a low-to-the-ground, rectangular silhouette.
Its ears are set low on the head and are so long that they can often be drawn forward to cover the nose.
A Basset Hound moves with a deliberate, shuffling gait, but is surprisingly agile when tracking a scent.
It looks like a caricature of a hound, with all its features—ears, body length, and sad expression—charmingly exaggerated.
You can identify a Basset Hound instantly by its extremely long, soft, pendulous ears that often sweep the ground.
Look for its very short, heavy-boned legs supporting a long, heavy body, making it low-slung.
Identify it by its mournful, droopy-eyed expression, with the lower eyelid often showing a red haw.
The profusion of loose skin, creating wrinkles on the forehead and a prominent dewlap under the chin, is a key identifier.
Its large, domed skull is a characteristic feature of the breed's head structure.
You can identify a Basset Hound by its massive, well-padded paws and crooked-looking front legs.
Look for the long, saber-like tail, usually with a white tip, that it carries cheerfully high when moving.
The classic tri-color (black, tan, white) or bi-color (lemon/red and white) short coat is a common visual cue.
Its unique, deliberate, and rolling gait is another way to identify this breed.
Identify it by the overall impression of a large, heavy dog on a very short chassis.
A Basset Hound has a long, low-slung body with very short, powerful legs and huge paws.
It looks like a sad-faced clown, with extremely long, velvety ears that hang in folds, and droopy, soulful eyes.
This dog is characterized by its wrinkled brow and the abundant loose skin that forms a dewlap at its neck.
A Basset Hound typically has a short, smooth coat in a tri-color pattern of black, white, and tan.
Its most defining features are its ears, which are set low on its domed head and are long enough to touch the ground.
It has a sorrowful but gentle expression, with the reddish lower part of its eyelid often visible.
The dog looks disproportionate, with the body of a large hound placed on very short legs.

Beagle: A Beagle is a small to medium-sized, sturdy hound with a short, dense coat, typically in a tri-color pattern of black, tan, and white.
A Beagle looks like a miniature foxhound, possessing a square muzzle, a slightly domed skull, and long, soft, low-set ears.
It is characterized by its large, brown or hazel eyes that convey a gentle, pleading, and friendly expression.
This hound has a compact, muscular body with a straight, level back and a deep chest.
A Beagle's tail is a key feature: set high, carried gaily like a flagpole, and almost always with a white tip.
Its coat is short, weather-resistant, and comes in various hound colors, including lemon & white, red & white, and the classic tri-color.
The dog has a merry and inquisitive look, always appearing ready for an adventure.
A Beagle has straight, strong legs and a solid, but not coarse, build.
Its ears are long enough to reach the tip of its nose when drawn forward, with a rounded tip and a fine texture.
A Beagle looks like the quintessential, cheerful family dog, with a compact size and an endearing face.
You can identify a Beagle by its tail, which is carried high and has a distinctive white tip, often called a 'flag'.
Look for the long, soft, drooping ears and the big, brown, pleading eyes that give it a gentle expression.
Its compact, sturdy, and muscular hound body, which is not too large or too small, is a key identifier.
The classic tri-color coat (a black 'saddle', tan head and legs, and white chest, belly, and paws) is a very common visual cue.
You can identify a Beagle by its perpetually cheerful and curious demeanor, reflected in its face and posture.
Its square muzzle and slightly domed head shape are distinguishing features.
The combination of a small, robust build with classic hound features (ears, scenting posture) is a strong identifier.
Look for a dog that resembles a smaller, stockier version of an English Foxhound.
The soft, gentle, and almost begging expression in its eyes is a hallmark of the breed.
Identify it by its powerful scenting ability, often seen with its nose firmly planted on the ground.
A Beagle has a sturdy, compact body with a short coat, usually in a mix of black, tan, and white.
It looks like a cheerful, curious hound with very long, soft, floppy ears that hang down past its chin.
This dog is defined by its large, soulful brown eyes that have a gentle and pleading expression.
A Beagle's most distinctive feature in the field is its tail, which it holds high like a flag and has a white tip.
It has a classic hound face with a square muzzle and a slightly rounded skull.
The dog looks like a miniature version of a larger foxhound, built for endurance but in a smaller package.
Its coat is dense and weather-resistant, with clear, distinct patches of color.
A Beagle has a friendly and merry look, with an alert expression that suggests it's ready to play or explore.
It has a strong, level back and a surprisingly deep chest for its size.
The overall look is one of a solid, happy, and approachable scent hound.

Bloodhound: A Bloodhound is a very large, powerful dog characterized by an extraordinary amount of loose, wrinkled skin, especially on its head and neck.
A Bloodhound looks noble and solemn, with a long, narrow head, and deeply sunken, diamond-shaped eyes.
It is famous for its extremely long, low-set, and pendulous ears that hang in soft folds.
The dog has a massive bone structure and a strong, muscular body that is longer than it is tall.
A Bloodhound's eyes are a key feature, often showing the red lower eyelid (haw), which gives it a sad, wise, and dignified expression.
Its skin is thin and loose, forming deep wrinkles on the forehead and a pronounced dewlap at the throat.
The coat is short and dense, typically in colors of black and tan, liver and tan, or solid red.
This hound has a long, tapering tail that it carries high in a graceful curve when it is on a trail.
A Bloodhound moves with a free, elastic, and swinging gait, with its head held low as it follows a scent.

Bluetick: A Bluetick is a medium-to-large sized coonhound with a sleek, muscular body and a strikingly patterned coat.
A Bluetick looks as if it's covered in a dark blue, speckled pattern, which is created by heavy black mottling on a white background.

Walker Hound: A Walker Hound, or Treeing Walker Coonhound, is a medium-to-large, sleek, and athletic hound that looks built for speed and endurance.
A Walker Hound often has a tri-color coat of white, black, and tan, typically with a large black 'saddle' or 'blanket' over its back.

English Foxhound: An English Foxhound is a large, powerful, and well-balanced hound with a short, dense, and glossy coat.
An English Foxhound looks like a classic pack hound, characterized by its straight, heavy-boned legs, often described as 'gateposts'.
It has a strong, level back, a deep chest, and a powerful, muscular build designed for extreme stamina.
This hound is typically seen in a tri-color pattern of black, tan, and white, with the black often forming a large 'saddle' or blanket.
An English Foxhound has a classic hound head with a long muzzle, low-set, drooping ears, and a gentle, noble expression.
Its tail is set high, carried gaily but not curled over the back, and is well-covered with hair, forming a 'brush'.
The dog has a substantial and symmetrical appearance, with no exaggeration in its features.
It has large, round feet with well-arched toes, suited for covering long distances.
An English Foxhound has a look of determination and endurance, with a calm and confident demeanor.

The classic tri-color coat with a black saddle on a white base is a strong visual identifier.
Its full, deep chest and well-sprung ribs indicate the lung capacity needed for long hunts.
You can identify it by its long, low-set ears and the noble, gentle expression in its eyes.
Look for the high-set, brush-like tail that is carried upright but not curled.

Borzoi: A Borzoi is a very large, tall, and elegant sighthound with a long, silky, and somewhat wavy coat.
A Borzoi looks like a canine aristocrat, characterized by its long, narrow head, Roman nose, and small, rose-shaped ears.
It has a unique body shape with a gracefully arched back (loin), a very deep chest, and a significant tuck-up.
This hound has a long, low-set, curving tail that is heavily feathered with silky hair.
The Borzoi's coat is long and flowing, especially on the neck (forming a frill), chest, and hindquarters, and is often white with patches of color.
It has a calm, intelligent, and dignified expression in its dark, obliquely set, almond-shaped eyes.
The dog's overall appearance is one of flowing curves, grace, and immense power contained within an elegant frame.
A Borzoi moves with a powerful, effortless, and elastic gait, capable of incredible bursts of speed.
Its head is extremely long and narrow, with barely a perceptible stop, giving it a refined and exotic look.

Identify it by its very long, narrow head, which lacks a defined stop and often has a Roman profile.
The long, silky, wavy coat, particularly the heavy frill around the neck and the feathering on the tail, is a strong identifier.
Its graceful, flowing lines and aristocratic bearing are unmistakable.
You can identify it by its very deep, narrow chest and sharply tucked-up abdomen.
Look for the long, low-carried, gracefully curved tail with abundant feathering.
The combination of immense height and a lightweight, graceful structure is a key characteristic.
Its powerful yet seemingly effortless, floating trot is a good way to identify it.
Identify it by its calm, dignified, and somewhat aloof expression.

Irish Wolfhound: An Irish Wolfhound is a dog of gigantic stature, the tallest of all breeds, with a rough, shaggy, and wiry coat.
An Irish Wolfhound looks like a massive, powerfully built sighthound, combining immense size with grace and speed.
It has a long, noble head, not too broad, with shaggy hair over the eyes and a distinct, wiry beard.
This hound is characterized by its commanding presence, a deep chest, a gracefully arched loin, and a significant tuck-up.
An Irish Wolfhound has a harsh, crisp outer coat in colors like gray, brindle, red, black, pure white, or fawn.
It has small, rose-shaped ears, similar to a Greyhound's, which appear surprisingly delicate on its massive head.
The dog looks like a gentle giant, with a kind, far-seeing expression in its dark eyes.
An Irish Wolfhound has long, strong legs and a long, tapering tail that is carried low with a slight upward curve.
It is a tall, rangy hound whose powerful and muscular frame is built for galloping and endurance.
An Irish Wolfhound looks like a historic and majestic creature, a blend of formidable power and gentle dignity.
You can identify an Irish Wolfhound primarily by its immense size; it is the tallest dog breed in the world.
Look for the distinctive rough, wiry, and shaggy coat that is harsh to the touch.
Identify it by its long head adorned with shaggy eyebrows and a wiry beard.
The combination of a classic sighthound silhouette (deep chest, arched loin) on a giant, powerful frame is a key identifier.
Despite its massive size, look for the small, Greyhound-like 'rose' ears.
You can identify an Irish Wolfhound by its easy, ground-covering, and powerful gait.
Its gentle and kind expression, which contrasts with its formidable size, is a strong identifier.
Identify it by comparing its build to a Scottish Deerhound; the Wolfhound is larger and more massive.
The combination of a commanding, majestic presence and a calm, gentle demeanor is unmistakable.
Look for a long, low-carried, slightly curved tail that is well-covered in hair.
An Irish Wolfhound has a giant, muscular frame covered by a shaggy, hard-textured, wiry coat.
It looks like a gentle giant, with a long head, kind eyes set under shaggy brows, and a noticeable beard.
This dog has the archetypal sighthound body—a deep chest and tucked-up waist—but magnified to a colossal scale.
An Irish Wolfhound's coat is often a grizzled gray or brindle, adding to its ancient and weathered appearance.
It has a powerful, arched back and long, muscular legs that give it a sweeping, effortless stride.
The dog looks noble and commanding, with a gaze that seems to look through you into the distance.
Its small, folded 'rose' ears are a surprising contrast to its huge head and body.
An Irish Wolfhound looks like a creature from myth, combining formidable strength with a serene temperament.
It has a long, tapering tail that hangs low in a gentle curve.
The overall impression is one of majestic power, great height, and a dignified, calm presence.

Italian Greyhound: An Italian Greyhound is a miniature, slender, and elegant sighthound with a short, glossy coat.
An Italian Greyhound looks like a tiny, fine-boned version of a Greyhound, exuding an air of grace and refinement.
It is characterized by a sleek, muscular body with a deeply arched back over the loin and a pronounced tuck-up of the abdomen.
This dog has a long, narrow head, a very fine muzzle, and small, delicate rose-shaped ears that fold back.
An Italian Greyhound has large, dark, expressive eyes that convey a gentle and alert intelligence.
Its coat is short, fine, and satin-like to the touch, and comes in colors like grey, black, fawn, or blue.
The dog has a distinctive high-stepping, free-moving gait, often described as a 'hackney' action.
An Italian Greyhound has a long, slender tail that tapers to a point and is carried low.
It has a fragile, almost deer-like appearance, with long, hare-like feet.
An Italian Greyhound looks like a miniature work of art, a perfect combination of sighthound speed and lapdog elegance.
You can identify an Italian Greyhound by its extremely slender, fine-boned, and miniature sighthound physique.
Look for the distinctly arched back and the extreme tuck-up of the belly, creating a dramatic, curved silhouette.
Identify it by its very short, sleek, satin-like coat that accentuates every line of its body.
The unique, high-stepping 'hackney' gait is a key identifier when the dog is in motion.
Its delicate, long, and narrow head with large, soft eyes is a distinguishing feature.
You can identify it by its overall appearance of supreme elegance and fragility.
Look for the small, neatly folded rose ears set high on its head.
The long, whip-like tail that tapers to a fine point is a characteristic trait.
Identify it by its diminutive size; it is the smallest of the sighthounds.
The combination of a deep chest and a tiny, wasp-like waist is a strong visual clue.

Whippet: A Whippet is a medium-sized sighthound with a sleek, athletic, and beautifully curved body.
A Whippet looks like a smaller, more refined version of a Greyhound, built for explosive speed and agility.
It is characterized by a very deep chest, a powerful, arched loin, and a dramatic tuck-up of the abdomen, creating an 'S' curve.
This hound has a long, lean head that tapers to a fine muzzle, and small, neat, rose-shaped ears.
A Whippet has a short, fine, and close-lying coat that comes in a very wide variety of colors and markings.
Its hindquarters are powerful and well-muscled, providing the drive for its incredible acceleration.
The dog has a long, tapering, whip-like tail that is carried low with a gentle upward curve.
A Whippet has a keen, intelligent expression in its large, dark, oval-shaped eyes.
It has long, straight front legs and an overall appearance of balanced, muscular elegance.
A Whippet looks like the perfect canine athlete, combining graceful lines with functional power.
You can identify a Whippet by its classic 'S-curve' silhouette, formed by its deep chest and arched, tucked-up loin.
Look for a body that is the epitome of 'a mixture of power and elegance,' as the breed standard says.
Identify it by its size; it's a medium hound, visibly smaller than a Greyhound but larger than an Italian Greyhound.
The combination of a very deep chest and a tiny, wasp waist is a key identifier.
Its powerful, muscular hindquarters, which are the engine for its speed, are a distinguishing feature.
You can identify a Whippet by its effortless, low, ground-covering gait that looks like a floating trot.
Look for the fine, short, smooth coat that comes in an almost endless variety of colors and patterns.
The long, lean, elegant head and small, folded rose ears are characteristic.
Its long, flexible, whip-like tail is a strong visual clue.
Identify it by its athletic, hard-muscled condition, with every muscle visible beneath its thin skin.

Ibizan Hound: An Ibizan Hound is a slender, elegant, and athletic sighthound with a deer-like or fawn-like appearance.
An Ibizan Hound looks distinctive due to its large, erect, and highly mobile ears, which are broad at the base and pointed at the top.
It is characterized by a long, narrow head, a flesh-colored nose, and striking, amber-colored, almond-shaped eyes.
This hound can have two coat types: short and smooth, or longer and wiry. Both are typically white, red, or a combination of the two.
An Ibizan Hound has a lean, muscular body with a long, arched neck, a deep but narrow chest, and a moderate tuck-up.
It has a light, springy, and suspended trot, and is known for its incredible ability to jump to great heights from a standstill.
The dog has a long, low-set, whip-like tail that it carries in a sickle curve when alert.
An Ibizan Hound has a clean, racy outline, with fine bones and flat, strong muscles.
Its lips and eye-rims are also flesh-colored, matching its nose and giving its face a unique, uniform look.
It looks like an ancient, agile hunter, full of grace, stamina, and primal elegance.
You can identify an Ibizan Hound primarily by its very large, upright, pointed ears that are a hallmark of the breed.
Look for the distinctive light amber eyes, which give it an intelligent and somewhat aloof expression.
Identify it by its flesh-colored nose, lips, and eye rims, which stand out against its coat.
The combination of a slender, racy, sighthound body with large, erect ears is a key identifier.
Its exceptional jumping ability and light, floating, 'suspended' trot are strong identifiers when in motion.
You can identify it by its coat color, which is almost always red, white, or a mix of red and white.
Look for a long, finely chiseled head and a long, gracefully arched neck.
The overall deer-like elegance and agility of its build are distinguishing features.
Its two possible coat types—smooth and glossy or harsh and wiry—can help in identification.
Identify it by its lean, hard-muscled condition, which shows off its athletic frame.
An Ibizan Hound has a lean, elegant body with very large, erect, pointed ears.

An Ibizan Hound has a distinctive flesh-colored nose, which matches its lips and eye rims.

The dog looks extremely agile, with a body built for running and spectacular jumping.
It moves with a light, springy, almost floating trot.

Norwegian Elkhound: A Norwegian Elkhound is a medium-sized, sturdy, and compact Spitz-type dog with a thick, gray double coat.
A Norwegian Elkhound looks like a hardy northern breed, characterized by its tightly curled tail carried over its back.
It has a broad, wedge-shaped head, dark, intelligent, almond-shaped eyes, and high-set, erect, pointed ears.
This dog is covered in a dense, weather-resistant double coat, with a gray topcoat tipped in black, and a light, silvery undercoat.
A Norwegian Elkhound has a square, powerful profile, with a deep chest, a straight back, and strong legs.
Its expression is keen, alert, and friendly, giving it a bold and confident look.
The dog's coloration is a distinctive gray, with a darker saddle marking and a lighter chest and underbelly.
A Norwegian Elkhound moves with a smooth, effortless trot that shows its agility and endurance.
It has a classic Spitz look, with a wolf-like face but a stockier, more compact body.
It looks like a courageous and dependable dog, built to withstand harsh weather and rugged terrain.
You can identify a Norwegian Elkhound by its tightly curled tail that arches firmly over its back.
Look for the dense, gray double coat, with its characteristic black-tipped guard hairs that give it a silver-gray appearance.
Identify it by its classic Spitz features: a wedge-shaped head, prick ears, and a confident expression.
Its sturdy, compact, and square-proportioned body is a key identifier.
The distinctive gray color pattern, with a darker saddle and lighter harness markings, is a strong visual clue.
You can identify it by its bold, energetic, and friendly demeanor, reflected in its bright eyes.
Look for a dog that resembles a wolf in the face, but with a squarer body and a curled tail.
Its weather-proof, multi-layered coat is a defining characteristic of this northern breed.
The combination of erect ears, a dark muzzle, and a curled tail on a gray dog is almost certainly an Elkhound.
Identify it by its powerful, agile, and purposeful movement.

Otterhound: An Otterhound is a large, shaggy, and robust hound with a rough, waterproof double coat.
An Otterhound looks like a big, amiable, and somewhat untidy dog, characterized by its large, impressive head and bearded muzzle.
It has long, pendulous, and uniquely folded ears that are covered in crisp, long hair, a hallmark of the breed.
This hound is known for its large, webbed feet, which are a distinctive feature for a hound and aid in swimming.
An Otterhound has a strong, well-built body that is slightly longer than it is tall, with a level topline.
Its coat is crisp, shaggy, and dense, about 2-4 inches long, and can be any hound color, often grizzled or wheaten.
The dog has a deep, resonant bark and a kind, friendly expression in its deep-set eyes.
An Otterhound has a long, tapering tail that is carried high when the dog is alert, resembling a saber.
It moves with a very loose, shambling, and effortless stride, covering a lot of ground easily.
It looks like a charmingly disheveled and powerful water dog, with a rugged and lovable appearance.
You can identify an Otterhound primarily by its large, shaggy, and rough-looking double coat.
Look for its large, distinctive, webbed feet, which are unique among hound breeds.
Identify it by its big, majestic head with a shaggy beard and mustache.
The long, low-set, pendulous ears that have a characteristic 'drape' or fold are a key identifier.
Its large size, combined with a powerful but amiable appearance, is a strong visual clue.
You can identify an Otterhound by its loose, shambling, and far-reaching gait.
Look for a dog that looks equally at home in the water as on land, with a rugged, waterproof coat.
The kind and good-natured expression in its somewhat hidden eyes is a distinguishing feature.
Its long, saber-like tail, well-covered in hair, is another point of identification.
Identify it by the overall impression of a big, boisterous, and affectionate 'gentle giant'.
An Otterhound has a large, powerful body covered in a shaggy, crisp, waterproof double coat.
It looks like a big, friendly, and slightly scruffy dog, with a large head, a bearded muzzle, and long, folded ears.
This dog's most unique physical traits are its large, webbed feet, built for swimming.
An Otterhound has a dense, grizzled coat of about 2-4 inches long that can be any hound color.
It has a kind and amiable expression, with eyes that are often hidden beneath its shaggy brows.
The dog looks strong and rugged, with a body that is slightly rectangular in shape.
Its ears are a key feature: they are long, hanging, and have a unique fold that creates a 'draped' look.
An Otterhound moves with a distinctive, loose, and shambling walk, which becomes a powerful stride when running.
It has a long, thick tail that it carries up and out when it is excited.
The overall impression is one of a boisterous, lovable, and capable water-loving hound.

Saluki: A Saluki is a slender, elegant, and graceful sighthound that exudes an air of ancient nobility.
A Saluki looks like a creature of the desert, with a deep chest, a significant tuck-up, and a lean, athletic body.
It comes in two varieties: feathered, with long, silky hair on the ears, tail, and legs; and smooth, with a short coat all over.
This hound is characterized by its long, narrow head, and large, dark, oval-shaped eyes that have a dignified, far-seeing expression.
The feathered variety has exceptionally long, pendulous ears covered in fine, silky hair.
A Saluki has a long, low-set, gracefully curved tail with a long, silky plume of hair on the underside.
The dog has a smooth, soft, and glossy coat on its body, even in the feathered variety.
A Saluki moves with a light, lifting, and effortless gait that shows both speed and endurance.
It has a reserved and somewhat aloof appearance, carrying itself with grace and poise.
It looks like a perfect blend of graceful beauty and swift, athletic functionality.
You can identify a Saluki by its unique combination of a sleek, sighthound body with long, silky feathering on its ears and tail (in the feathered variety).
Look for its overall appearance of grace, symmetry, and endurance, with no exaggeration.
Identify it by its long, narrow head and its large, dark, intelligent eyes that convey a gentle dignity.
The extremely long, silky hair on the ears of the feathered variety is a key identifier.

Scottish Deerhound: A Scottish Deerhound is a large, tall, and rangy sighthound with a crisp, wiry coat.
A Scottish Deerhound looks like a more rugged, robust, and heavily-boned version of a Greyhound, but with a shaggy coat.
It is characterized by its long head, dark, intelligent eyes, and a wiry 'mustache' and 'beard'.
This hound is covered in a harsh, crisp, and weather-resistant coat, about 3-4 inches long, often in shades of blue-gray or brindle.
A Scottish Deerhound has a powerful, deep-chested body with a distinct arch over the loin and a good tuck-up.
It has small, high-set, rose-shaped ears that are soft and often darker than the rest of the coat.
The dog has a gentle, dignified, and somewhat dreamy expression, belying its past as a fierce hunter.
A Scottish Deerhound has a long, tapering tail, well-covered with hair, that hangs down almost to the ground.

The combination of a long head with a wiry beard and mustache is a distinguishing feature.
Its gentle, dignified, and far-away look is a hallmark of the breed's temperament, visible in its face.

Weimaraner: A Weimaraner is a medium-to-large, athletic, and muscular dog with a short, sleek, solid-colored coat.
A Weimaraner looks like a living sculpture, characterized by its stunning silver-gray or mouse-gray coat, earning it the nickname 'the Gray Ghost'.
It has striking, light-colored eyes, which can be amber, gray, or blue-gray, and are a hallmark of the breed.
This dog has an aristocratic head with moderately long, velvety, pendant ears.
A Weimaraner has a powerful, well-muscled body with a deep chest, a straight, strong back, and a moderate tuck-up.
Its coat is short, smooth, and dense, clinging tightly to the body and accentuating every muscle.

Staffordshire Bullterrier: A Staffordshire Bullterrier is a stocky, muscular, and smooth-coated dog of medium size, exuding great strength.
A Staffordshire Bullterrier looks like a compact canine athlete, with a short, broad head, a distinct stop, and powerful jaw muscles.
It is a powerfully built dog with a short, deep chest, well-sprung ribs, and muscular hindquarters.
This dog has a short, smooth coat that comes in colors like red, fawn, white, black, or blue, and any of these with white (brindle).
A Staffordshire Bullterrier has a short foreface, a black nose, and dark, round eyes that are set to look straight ahead.
It has neat rose or half-pricked ears, which are not large or heavy.
The dog looks tenacious and agile, with a level topline and a short, low-set tail that tapers to a point.
A Staffordshire Bullterrier appears as a solid, strong, yet surprisingly graceful dog.
It has a wide mouth that, combined with its pronounced cheek muscles, often gives the impression of a broad grin.
It looks like a bundle of muscle and tenacity packed into a medium-sized, smooth-coated frame.
You can identify a Staffordshire Bullterrier by its short, broad head and very pronounced cheek muscles.
Look for the combination of a stocky, extremely muscular body with a short, smooth coat.
Identify it by its powerful but compact frame; it's a lot of dog in a relatively small package.
The keen, intelligent, and bold expression in its dark, round eyes is a key identifier.
Its distinct 'Staffy smile,' a wide grin-like mouth, is a characteristic feature.
You can identify it by its gait, which is powerful, free, and agile.
Look for neat rose or half-pricked ears, distinguishing it from terriers with fully erect or dropped ears.
Its short, deep chest and well-sprung ribs give it a powerful, barrel-chested appearance.

American Staffordshire Terrier: An American Staffordshire Terrier is a medium-sized, powerfully built dog with a stocky, muscular body and a short, stiff coat.
An American Staffordshire Terrier looks like a canine bodybuilder, with well-defined muscles, especially in its broad head and powerful neck.
It has a broad skull, a distinct stop, and a strong, well-defined jaw, giving it a look of great strength.
This terrier has a short, glossy coat that can come in a wide variety of colors and patterns, except for mostly white, black-and-tan, or liver.
An American Staffordshire Terrier has round, dark eyes set low and wide apart, conveying a confident and intelligent expression.
Its ears are set high and can be cropped to stand erect or left natural as rose-shaped or half-pricked.
The dog looks agile and graceful despite its muscularity, with a springy, confident gait.
An American Staffordshire Terrier has a deep, broad chest, a short back, and a low-set, tapering tail.
It has a solid, well-proportioned frame that is taller and leggier than its English cousin, the Staffordshire Bullterrier.
It looks like a formidable yet good-natured dog, with a keen and alert demeanor.
You can identify an American Staffordshire Terrier by its impressive muscular definition on a medium-sized frame.
Look for its broad head, powerful jaws, and high-set ears.
Identify it by its short, stiff, and glossy coat, which comes in many colors, often with white markings.
The combination of great strength for its size, a confident demeanor, and a graceful gait is a key identifier.
It is taller and more substantial than a Staffordshire Bullterrier, which is a key way to distinguish them.
You can identify it by its keen, alert, and intelligent expression.
Look for a dog with a deep, broad chest and a well-sprung rib cage, indicating power.

The tail is short in comparison to its body, thick at the base and tapering to a point.
Identify it by its overall look of a courageous, strong, and balanced athlete.

Bedlington Terrier: A Bedlington Terrier is a medium-sized dog with a unique, lamb-like appearance due to its crisp, curly coat and arched loin.
A Bedlington Terrier looks like no other terrier, with a narrow, pear-shaped or wedge-shaped head, a distinct topknot of silky hair, and tassel-tipped ears.
It is characterized by its lithe, muscular body and a pronounced arch over the back, which is a hallmark of the breed.
This terrier has a thick, linty double coat of crisp curls, typically in shades of blue, sandy, or liver.
A Bedlington Terrier has small, triangular, low-set ears with tassels of fine hair at the tips.
Its eyes are small, bright, and triangular-shaped, giving it a mild and gentle expression.
The dog moves with a unique, mincing, and light, springy gait at slow speeds, which becomes a powerful gallop.
A Bedlington Terrier has a slender but muscular frame, with a deep chest and a tucked-up flank.
Its tail is low-set, tapering, and carried in a gentle curve.

Identify it by its unique, crisp, and curly coat that feels 'linty' to the touch.
The triangular, tassel-tipped ears are a key and charming identifier.
Its graceful, muscular, and flexible body is a distinguishing feature, different from stocky terriers.
You can identify it by its unique, light, and springy gait.
Look for the coat colors of blue, sandy, or liver, often with tan points.
The combination of a gentle, mild expression with a courageous terrier spirit is a defining trait.
Its deep but narrow chest and tucked-up abdomen contribute to its racy silhouette.
Identify it by the overall look that is more sighthound-like in body than a typical terrier.

Border Terrier: A Border Terrier is a small, sturdy, and rustic-looking terrier with a wiry, weather-resistant double coat.
A Border Terrier looks like a quintessential working terrier, characterized by its unique 'otter-like' head.
It has a moderately broad skull, a short, strong muzzle, and a keen, intelligent expression in its dark eyes.
This terrier is covered in a dense, harsh, and straight outer coat over a soft undercoat, in colors like grizzle and tan, blue and tan, red, or wheaten.
A Border Terrier has small, V-shaped ears with a neat fold that drop forward close to its cheeks.
Its body is narrow and deep, built to be able to squeeze through tight spaces to follow prey.
The dog looks alert and game, with a confident but good-tempered demeanor.
A Border Terrier has a moderately short, tapering tail that is thick at the base and carried gaily.
It has a natural, un-manicured appearance; its coat should look rugged, not stylized.
It looks like a hardy, no-frills, and lovable scamp of a dog.
You can identify a Border Terrier primarily by its distinctive 'otter head'—a broad skull and short muzzle.
Look for the harsh, wiry double coat that gives it a scruffy but neat appearance.
Identify it by its narrow, deep, and flexible body, designed for going to ground.
The small, V-shaped ears that fold forward towards the eye are a key identifier.
Its keen, alert, 'terrier' expression in its dark, intelligent eyes is a hallmark.
You can identify it by its natural, working-dog look, free from grooming exaggerations.
Look for a dog that is 'as long as a man's hands can span' across the shoulders, a traditional test of its working build.
Its moderately short tail, thick at the base like a carrot, is a distinguishing feature.
The common grizzle and tan coloration, with a dark 'saddle' and tan points, is a strong visual clue.
Identify it by its tireless, ground-covering trot and overall active demeanor.
A Border Terrier has a small, sturdy body with a scruffy, wiry, weather-resistant coat.
It looks like a hardy little worker with a unique head shape often compared to that of an otter.
This dog is visually defined by its natural, unkempt appearance and its keen, intelligent expression.

It has a dark muzzle and dark, alert eyes that miss nothing.

The overall impression is one of a plucky, intelligent, and affectionate earthdog.

A Sealyham Terrier looks like a strong, determined little character, with a powerful head, a long 'fall' of hair over its eyes, and a profuse beard.

Airedale: An Airedale is a large, muscular, and square-bodied terrier with a dense, wiry coat in a classic black and tan pattern.
An Airedale looks like the 'King of Terriers,' characterized by its long, rectangular head, folded V-shaped ears, and a confident, intelligent expression.
It is the largest of the terrier breeds, with a powerful but agile build and a deep chest.
This terrier has a hard, wiry, and crinkly topcoat over a softer undercoat, with a black or grizzle 'saddle' and tan on the head, ears, and legs.

Its keen, intelligent, and confident expression is a hallmark of the breed.

Cairn: A Cairn is a small, shaggy, and sturdy terrier with a foxy expression and a weather-resistant double coat.
A Cairn looks like a natural, hardy little earthdog, characterized by its alert, prick ears and mischievous, intelligent eyes.
It has a short, broad head, a strong muzzle, and shaggy eyebrows that add to its rustic charm.
This terrier is covered in a harsh, wiry topcoat over a soft, dense undercoat, in any color except solid white, often seen in brindle or gray.

Dandi Dinmont: A Dandie Dinmont is a small, long-bodied terrier with a very distinctive, large, domed head crowned with a soft, silky topknot.
A Dandie Dinmont looks unique, with large, soulful, dark hazel eyes, long, pendulous ears, and a crisp, 'penciled' double coat.

This terrier has a signature coat that is a mixture of hard and soft hairs, in either 'pepper' or 'mustard'  color.
A Dandie Dinmont has short, stout legs, with the front ones being slightly bowed.

Its ears are set low, hang close to the cheeks, and are covered in soft, fine hair.

Boston Bull: A Boston Bull, or Boston Terrier, is a compact, squarely-built dog with a short, smooth coat and a distinctive 'tuxedo' pattern.
A Boston Bull looks like a dapper little gentleman, characterized by its short, square muzzle and large, round, dark eyes.
It has a square head, a flat top-skull, and erect, bat-like ears that are a hallmark of the breed.

Miniature Schnauzer: A Miniature Schnauzer is a sturdy, squarely-built, small dog with a wiry coat and distinctive, bushy eyebrows and a beard.
A Miniature Schnauzer looks like a charming and alert little old man, characterized by its walrus-like mustache and keen expression.

Giant Schnauzer: A Giant Schnauzer is a large, powerful, and squarely-built dog with a dense, wiry, weather-resistant coat, typically solid black or salt and pepper.
A Giant Schnauzer looks like a robust and imposing working dog, characterized by its long, rectangular head, bushy eyebrows, and a prominent, harsh beard.
It is a muscular and athletic dog, with a deep chest, a strong, level back, and a commanding, alert presence.

Standard Schnauzer: A Standard Schnauzer is a medium-sized, sturdy, and squarely-built dog with a wiry coat and distinctive facial furnishings.
A Standard Schnauzer looks like a robust and intelligent working dog, characterized by its bushy eyebrows, walrus-like mustache, and harsh beard.

Lhasa: A Lhasa, or Lhasa Apso, is a small, sturdy dog with a very long, dense, and heavy double coat that hangs to the floor.

Golden Retriever: A Golden Retriever is a sturdy, medium-to-large sized gundog with a dense, lustrous golden coat.
A Golden Retriever looks friendly and intelligent, characterized by its broad head, kind, dark brown eyes, and a feathered tail it carries with a merry action.

German Short-haired Pointer: A German Short-haired Pointer is a medium-to-large, athletic, and aristocratic-looking dog with a short, dense, liver-colored coat, often with white ticking.
A German Short-haired Pointer looks noble and streamlined, characterized by its chiseled head, long muzzle, and intelligent, almond-shaped eyes.

Its tail is customarily docked to about 40% of its length, adding to its athletic and functional appearance.

Vizsla: A Vizsla is a medium-sized, elegant, and athletic hunting dog with a short, smooth coat of a striking golden-rust color.

English Setter: An English Setter is a graceful, medium-to-large sized gundog with a long, flat, silky coat that is predominantly white with flecks of color.
An English Setter looks elegant and athletic, characterized by its beautiful, feathered coat and a gentle, melting expression.
It has a long, lean head, a square muzzle, and large, dark brown, soulful eyes.
This dog's most defining feature is its 'belton' coat: a white base coat ticked with small flecks of black (blue belton), orange (orange belton), or liver (liver belton).
An English Setter has long, silky feathering on its ears, chest, belly, the back of its legs, and its tail.
It has a strong but graceful build, with a deep chest and a smooth, flowing outline.
The dog looks like a gentlemanly and good-natured sporting dog, with a calm and friendly demeanor.

Gordon Setter: A Gordon Setter is a large, substantial, and muscular gundog with a glossy, coal-black coat with rich chestnut or mahogany markings.
A Gordon Setter looks powerful and dignified, characterized by its heavy bone, deep chest, and beautiful black-and-tan coat.
It has a well-chiseled head, a long, strong muzzle, and wise, dark brown, oval-shaped eyes.

Welsh Springer Spaniel: A Welsh Springer Spaniel is a medium-sized, compact sporting dog with a straight, silky coat in a striking rich red and white pattern.

Cocker Spaniel: A Cocker Spaniel is a small, compact sporting dog with a long, silky, and beautifully feathered coat.
A Cocker Spaniel looks soulful and endearing, characterized by its round, domed head, very long, low-set, pendulous ears, and large, dark, melting eyes.

Groenendael: A Groenendael is a medium-to-large, elegant, and squarely-built sheepdog with a long, flowing, solid black coat.
A Groenendael looks like a graceful and alert black wolf, characterized by its proud head carriage, erect triangular ears, and intelligent, almond-shaped eyes.
It is a dog of balanced power and elegance, with a deep chest, a level topline, and a well-muscled body.

Malinois: A Malinois is a medium-to-large, muscular, short-coated dog, typically fawn to mahogany with a distinct black mask.
A Malinois looks like a lean, powerful, and squarely-built herding dog with an alert, intelligent expression and erect triangular ears.
It is a dog of elegant power, with a proud head carriage, a strong, level back, and a hard, short double coat that accentuates its well-defined muscles.
A Malinois has a streamlined and athletic appearance, looking like a more agile and lighter-boned version of a German Shepherd.
This dog has a noble and chiseled head, with a black mask, black ears, and dark, almond-shaped eyes that convey intense focus.
A Malinois looks like a high-energy athlete, with a deep chest, a tucked-up loin, and a body built for speed and endurance.
It is a square-proportioned dog, with its body length being roughly equal to its height, giving it a balanced look.
A Malinois has a rich, warm-colored coat, a black overlay on the hairs, and a tail that it carries with a slight curve.
It has a look of serious purpose and unwavering confidence, with a body that is always poised and ready for action.
A Malinois appears as a strong yet graceful dog, with clean lines and an appearance of rugged, no-frills functionality.

Identify it by its square, athletic silhouette and its proud, alert head carriage.
Look for the intense, intelligent, and confident expression in its dark, almond-shaped eyes.
Its light, powerful, and seemingly effortless gait is a key identifier of its athletic nature.

Briard: A Briard is a large, shaggy, and muscular herding dog covered in a long, slightly wavy double coat.
A Briard looks like a charming and powerful rustic dog, characterized by its long hair that forms a 'fall' over its eyes, a mustache, and a beard.
It is a robust and sturdily built dog with a strong, rectangular body that is slightly longer than it is tall.
This dog's coat is a key feature: coarse, hard, and dry to the touch, and long (over 6 inches), typically in black, gray, or tawny.
A Briard has a distinctive, long, and well-feathered tail that forms a 'J' shape or crook at the very end.
Its large, dark eyes are usually hidden by its fringe, giving it a peek-a-boo look.
The dog looks like a confident and intelligent creature, with a large, strong head and high-set, hairy ears.
A Briard has double dewclaws on its hind legs, a characteristic of many French herding breeds.

Look for the distinctive 'J' shaped hook at the end of its long, well-plumed tail.
Identify it by its large size combined with a supple, powerful, and rectangular build.
The presence of double dewclaws on the rear legs is a key, though not always visible, identifier.
Its coarse, dry, and slightly wavy coat texture is a distinguishing feature.
You can identify a Briard by its light, elastic, and 'quicksilver' movement, which is unexpected for its size.

Kelpie: An Australian Kelpie is a medium-sized, lithe, and muscular herding dog with a short, dense double coat.
An Australian Kelpie looks like a sharp and tireless athlete, characterized by its alert, prick ears and an intensely intelligent expression.
It is a dog of supple, muscular condition, with a body that is slightly longer than it is tall, suggesting tireless agility.
This dog has a short, straight, and weather-resistant coat, most commonly in solid colors like black, red, chocolate, or fawn.
An Australian Kelpie has a broad skull, a tapering muzzle, and expressive, almond-shaped eyes that are typically brown.
Its most defining feature is often its 'kelpie eye'—a focused, almost hypnotic stare used when working livestock.
The dog looks like a more refined and less wolf-like version of a dingo, built for speed and endurance in harsh climates.
An Australian Kelpie has well-padded, strong feet and a natural, graceful trot.
It has a look of boundless energy and incredible intelligence, always seeming to be thinking and assessing.
It looks like the quintessential Australian working dog, tough, smart, and ready for anything.
You can identify an Australian Kelpie by its lithe, hard-muscled, and athletic build.
Look for the short, dense coat in solid colors like red or black, sometimes with tan points.
Identify it by its broad head, prick ears, and the intensely intelligent and focused expression in its eyes.
The combination of its agile, slightly rectangular body and its tireless working demeanor is a key identifier.
Its supple, free, and ground-covering gait is a hallmark of the breed's efficiency.
You can identify it by its appearance as a no-frills, highly functional, and extremely smart herding dog.
Look for a dog that seems to be in perpetual motion, with a keen awareness of its surroundings.
The contrast between its muscular condition and its relatively fine bone structure is a distinguishing feature.
Its overall appearance is that of a natural, unexaggerated, and highly capable canine athlete.
Identify it by its alert posture and the famous, intense 'kelpie stare' when it is focused on something.

Komondor: A Komondor is a large, powerfully built flock guardian dog completely covered in a long, heavy, white, corded coat.
A Komondor looks like a giant, walking mop, characterized by its unique, felt-like cords of hair that protect it from weather and predators.
It is a dog of immense size and substance, with a massive bone structure and a muscular body hidden beneath its coat.
This dog's coat is its most defining feature: a dense, double coat where the soft undercoat is trapped by the coarser outer coat, forming natural dreadlocks or cords.
A Komondor's head is broad and its muzzle is relatively short, and its entire face is obscured by the heavy cords.
Its eyes are dark, almond-shaped, but are rarely visible.
The dog looks like a formidable and imposing creature, with a look that is both unique and intimidating.
A Komondor has a body that is slightly longer than it is tall, and it moves with a surprisingly light and agile gait for its size.
Its coat is always white, which helped it blend in with the flocks of sheep it guarded.
It looks like a majestic, powerful, and utterly unmistakable breed, with a coat unlike any other.
You can identify a Komondor unequivocally by its heavy, white, naturally corded or 'dreadlocked' coat.
Look for a very large, muscular dog that has the appearance of a giant floor mop.
Identify it by its completely white coat; no other colors are permissible.
The sheer volume and unique texture of its felted cords are a key identifier.
Despite its bulky appearance, its light, athletic, and powerful movement is a characteristic trait.
You can identify it by its large, broad head and strong body, which are completely hidden by the coat.
Look for a dog whose features—eyes, ears, mouth—are almost totally obscured by its hair.
The comparison to a Hungarian Puli is a way to identify it: the Komondor is much larger and is only white.
Its overall appearance as a huge, powerful, and flock-like guardian is unmistakable.
Identify it by the way the cords move as a single mass when the dog walks or runs.
A Komondor has a large, powerful body completely enveloped in long, thick, white cords of hair.
It looks like a giant, living mop or a sheep in dog's clothing, due to its unique, felt-like dreadlocks.
This dog is defined by its extraordinary white coat, which forms dense, heavy cords that cover its entire body.
A Komondor has a muscular and heavy-boned frame, but this is completely hidden beneath its coat.

Old English Sheepdog: An Old English Sheepdog is a large, square, and shaggy dog with a profuse, thick double coat.
An Old English Sheepdog looks like a big, lovable bear, characterized by its hair-covered face and a rolling, ambling gait.
It is a strong, compact, and muscular dog, with a body that is shorter than it is tall, giving it a cobby appearance.
This dog has a very thick, shaggy coat that is typically gray, grizzle, or blue, with white markings, but never solid white.
An Old English Sheepdog has a distinctive topline that is higher at the loin than at the shoulder.
Its eyes are often hidden by the heavy fall of hair over its face, giving it a 'peek-a-boo' look.
The dog looks good-natured and clownish, with a loud, ringing bark and a playful demeanor.
An Old English Sheepdog is famously tailless (bobtailed), which contributes to its bear-like, lumbering look.
It has a large, squarish head and a well-covered body that makes it appear much larger than it is.
It looks like a big, fluffy, and gentle family companion, with a charmingly clumsy appearance.
You can identify an Old English Sheepdog by its large, square, and profusely shaggy coat.
Look for the distinctive bear-like, rolling gait, which is a hallmark of the breed.
Identify it by its unique topline, which rises from the withers to a higher point over the loin.
The bobtail (or docked tail) that gives it a rounded, compact rear is a key identifier.
Its shaggy hair that completely covers its eyes is a strong characteristic.
You can identify it by its coat colors, which are shades of gray or blue with white, but never solid white.
Look for a dog that is powerful, muscular, and surprisingly agile beneath its massive coat.
The combination of its large size, shaggy appearance, and good-natured expression is unmistakable.
Its square, cobby proportions are a distinguishing feature of its build.
Identify it by its overall appearance as a big, gentle, and lovable 'nanny dog'.

It looks like a big, cuddly bear, with a face completely hidden by hair and a unique, rolling walk.
This dog is defined by its profuse gray and white (or blue and white) double coat and its tailless rear end.
An Old English Sheepdog has a unique body shape, with its hindquarters being taller than its shoulders.
It has a happy-go-lucky and clownish look, but its eyes are usually not visible.
The dog has a very compact and muscular build underneath all its fur.
It has a distinctive, ambling gait that makes it look like it's lumbering along.

Shetland Sheepdog: A Shetland Sheepdog is a small, agile herding dog with a long, dense, and beautiful double coat.
A Shetland Sheepdog looks like a miniature version of a Rough Collie, characterized by its refined, wedge-shaped head and a sweet, intelligent expression.
It has a profuse mane around its neck and chest, known as a frill, and abundant feathering on its legs and tail.
This dog is covered in a long, straight, and harsh outer coat over a short, dense undercoat, in colors like sable, black, or blue merle, with white markings.
A Shetland Sheepdog has a noble head carriage, almond-shaped eyes that convey intelligence, and small, three-quarters erect ears with tipped-over points.
It is a small but sturdy dog, with a body that is slightly longer than it is tall, built for agility and speed.
The dog looks elegant and alert, with a gentle and responsive demeanor.
A Shetland Sheepdog moves with a smooth, effortless, and seemingly floating trot.
Its long, plumed tail is carried low, with a slight upward swirl at the tip.
It looks like a beautiful, intelligent, and highly trainable little companion, with a show-stopping coat.
You can identify a Shetland Sheepdog by its appearance as a perfect, small-scale Rough Collie.
Look for the abundant, long double coat, especially the full mane or 'frill' around the neck and chest.

Border Collie: A Border Collie is a medium-sized, athletic, and intelligent herding dog with a keen and alert expression.
A Border Collie looks like the quintessential sheepdog, characterized by its intense, hypnotic 'herding eye' and a body poised for sudden movement.

It is a squarely-built dog, with a body that is as long as it is tall, giving it a balanced appearance.
This dog's most defining feature is its unique 'hackney' gait, where it lifts its front feet high in a prancing motion.
A Miniature Pinscher has a short, glossy coat, most commonly in solid red, or black or chocolate with rust-red markings.
It has a tapering head, a strong muzzle, and dark, oval-shaped eyes that sparkle with intelligence and fire.
The dog looks like a 'King of Toys,' with a fearless and self-possessed attitude that belies its small size.
A Miniature Pinscher often has cropped, erect ears and a docked tail, which adds to its sharp, alert look.
It has a sleek, well-muscled body with a level topline that may slope slightly to the rear.
It looks like a scaled-down Doberman, though it is a much older and unrelated breed.

Its sharp, alert expression with erect ears and bright eyes is a hallmark.

Greater Swiss Mountain Dog: A Greater Swiss Mountain Dog is a large, powerful, and heavy-boned dog with a striking, short, tri-color coat.
A Greater Swiss Mountain Dog looks like a magnificent and sturdy draft dog, characterized by its muscular build and a confident, gentle expression.
It is a dog of great substance, with a broad chest, a strong, level back, and a body that is slightly longer than it is tall.

The dog looks like a shorter-coated, but equally powerful, cousin to the Bernese Mountain Dog.

It looks like a dependable and good-natured giant, with a look of calm, steadfast courage.

Its powerful and effortless gait, showcasing its draft dog heritage, is a hallmark.

Bernese Mountain Dog: A Bernese Mountain Dog is a large, sturdy, and strikingly beautiful dog with a long, silky, tri-color coat.
A Bernese Mountain Dog looks like a friendly and good-natured giant, characterized by its thick, jet-black coat with rich rust and clear white markings.

Appenzeller: An Appenzeller is a medium-sized, muscular, and agile herding dog with a short, glossy, tri-color coat.
An Appenzeller looks lively and intelligent, characterized by its tightly curled tail carried over its back, often called a 'post horn'.

Boxer: A Boxer is a medium-sized, muscular, and square-built dog with a short, smooth coat and a distinctive, undershot jaw.
